
# Some important distribution models

```{js, echo=F}
function checkAnswer_mmc(q) {
      const selectedOptions = document.querySelectorAll('input[name="option'+q.id+'"]:checked');
      if (selectedOptions.length === 2) {
        const userAnswers = Array.from(selectedOptions).map(option => option.value);
        const correctAnswers = q.answer;
        const isCorrect = userAnswers.every(answer => correctAnswers.includes(answer));

if (isCorrect) {
          document.getElementById('result'+q.id).textContent = 'Congratulations! Your answer is correct.';
          document.getElementById('result'+q.id).style.color = "green";
        } else {
          document.getElementById('result'+q.id).textContent = `Sorry, incorrect.`;
          document.getElementById('result'+q.id).style.color = "red";
        }
      } else {
        document.getElementById('result'+q.id).textContent = 'Please select two options.';
      }
    }
// numeric
function generateQuestion_num(id) {
      // Generate two random numbers between 2 and 10
      const num1 = Math.floor(Math.random() * 10) + 2;
      const num2 = Math.floor(Math.random() * 10) + 2;
      const x1 = Math.floor(Math.random() * 100) + 2;
      const x2 = Math.floor(Math.random() * 100) + 2;
      const x3 = Math.floor(Math.random() * 100) + 2;
      const x4 = Math.floor(Math.random() * 100) + 2;
      const x5 = Math.floor(Math.random() * 100) + 2;
      const x6 = Math.floor(Math.random() * 100) + 2;

       // Calculate the correct answer
      let answer = num1*Math.sqrt(x1) + num2*Math.sqrt(x2);


      // Return the question and answer as an object
      return {
       // question: num1 + "\\(\\sqrt{x}+\\)"+ num2 + "\\(\\sqrt{y}\\)" + " for \\(x=\\)" + x1 + " and \\(y=\\)" + x2,
        question: [num1,num2,x1,x2,x3,x4,x5,x6],
        answer: answer,
        id: id
      };
    }

function checkAnswer_num(q) {
      const userAnswer = document.getElementById('answer'+q.id).value;
      const isCorrect = Math.round(parseFloat(userAnswer)*1000)/1000 === Math.round(q.answer*1000)/1000;

      if (isCorrect) {
        document.getElementById('result'+q.id).textContent = 'Congratulations! Your answer is correct.';
        document.getElementById('result'+q.id).style.color = "green";
      } else {
        document.getElementById('result'+q.id).textContent = `Sorry, incorrect. The correct answer is ` + Math.round(q.answer*10000)/10000 + '.';
        document.getElementById('result'+q.id).style.color = "red";
      }
  }

// plain multiple choice
  function checkAnswer_match(q) {
      const selectedOption = document.querySelector('input[name="option'+q.id+'"]:checked');
      if (selectedOption) {
        const userAnswer = selectedOption.value;
        const isCorrect = userAnswer === q.answer;

        if (isCorrect) {
          document.getElementById('result'+q.id).textContent = 'Congratulations! Your answer is correct.';
          document.getElementById('result'+q.id).style.color = "green";
        } else {
          document.getElementById('result'+q.id).textContent = `Sorry, incorrect.`;
          document.getElementById('result'+q.id).style.color = "red";
        }
      } else {
        document.getElementById('result'+q.id).textContent = 'Please select an option.';
      }
    }
    
function factorial(n){
  let answer = 1;
  if (n == 0 || n == 1){
    return answer;
  }
  else if(n > 1){
    for(var i = n; i >= 1; i--){
      answer = answer * i;
    }
    return answer;
  }
  else{
    return "number has to be positive."
  }  
}

function binomial(n, k) {
     if ((typeof n !== 'number') || (typeof k !== 'number')) 
  return false; 
    var coeff = 1;
    for (var x = n-k+1; x <= n; x++) coeff *= x;
    for (x = 1; x <= k; x++) coeff /= x;
    return coeff;
}

function binomialCDF(k,n,p) {
    let answer = 0;

    for(var i = 0; i <= k; i++){
      answer = answer + binomial(n,i)*Math.pow(p,i)*Math.pow((1-p),(n-i));
    }
    return answer;
}

function normalCDF(x, mean, std) {
  var x = (x - mean) / std
  var t = 1 / (1 + .2315419 * Math.abs(x))
  var d =.3989423 * Math.exp( -x * x / 2)
  var prob = d * t * (.3193815 + t * ( -.3565638 + t * (1.781478 + t * (-1.821256 + t * 1.330274))))
  if( x > 0 ) prob = 1 - prob
  return prob
}
```


```{r echo=FALSE, width="200%"}
#knitr::include_graphics("./images/housewife.png",dpi=100)#dpi=100
#knitr::include_graphics("./images/rockstar.png",dpi=90)#dpi=90#online version

#link="http://3.144.126.30:3838/"
link="http://learning-dashboard.lehre.hwr-berlin.de:3838/"# link to the apps
```

We have already modeled some random variables and it was always quite time-consuming to derive the distribution of random variables. We had to look at the original random process and determine for each result what value the random variable takes on for this result and derive the probability of the values based on that. Or with the continuous random variables, we had to laboriously calculate the distribution function, the expected value and the variance based on the density `r emoji::emoji("poop")`.

Luckily `r emoji::emoji("smiling_face_with_heart_eyes")` there are some pretty universal **distribution models** with variable parameters for which nice properties are already known and key figures can be determined using the parameters, so you don't have to use them every time must be derived again.

***

In this chapter we will learn:
  
- Which typical **distribution models** exist for discrete and continuous random variables?
- which **requirements** must be met so that a distribution model can be used in a specific case and
- how to determine the **parameters** of these distributions from the facts.

***
Since distributions associate values with probabilities, there are special distribution models for **discrete** random variables with discrete values and for **continuous** random variables that have intervals of sets of values.

## Discrete distributions

Discrete distributions describe probabilities concerning discrete random variables.

<!--
```{r,echo=F}
knitr::include_url(paste0(link,"Apps_RBook/#section-verteilungen"),
  height = "600px")
```
-->

```{r, echo=F, fig.cap="Distributional models for discrete random variables",width=640, height=600}
library(scales)
#disc uniform
n=5
par(mfrow=c(2,3))
  x<-seq(1,n)
  px<-rep(1/n,n)
  plot(x,px,type="h",xlab="x-values",ylab="f(x)",main=paste0("X~disc.Uniform(n=",n,")"),
       ylim=c(0,0.4),axes=F,lwd=5,col=scales::alpha("gray44",0.5))
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4))
  axis(1,at=x[c(1:3,n)], labels=c(expression(x[1]),expression(x[2]),"...",bquote(x[.(n)])))
  
#bern
  
input<-list()
input$pber<-0.6
 x<-c(0,1)
  px<-c(1-input$pber,input$pber)
  erw<-input$pber
  var<-input$pber*(1-input$pber)
  plot(x,px,type="h",xlab="k",ylab = "f(k)",main=paste0("X~Ber(p=",input$pber,")"),ylim=c(0,1),xlim=c(-0.5,1.5),axes=F,lwd=5,col=alpha("gray44",0.3))
  #rownames(bp)<-x
  bpm<-erw#*bp[rownames(bp)==floor(erw)]/floor(erw)
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.5,1))
  axis(1,at=c(0,1))
  abline(v=erw,lty=2,col=2)
  text(erw,0.9,
       paste0("E(X)=",round(erw,4)),col=2)
  arrows(0.1,0.5,
         0.9,0.5,lty=1,
         code=3,col=3, length=0.15,angle=15)
  text(erw,0.6,paste0("Var(X)=",round(var,4)),col=3)
  
  #binom
  input$nbin=10
  input$pbin=0.6
   x<-seq(0,input$nbin,1)
  px<-dbinom(x,size=input$nbin,prob=input$pbin)
  erw<-input$nbin*input$pbin
  #bp<-barplot(px,names.arg=x,xlab="k",main=paste0("X~B(n=",input$nbin,"; p=",input$pbin,")"),ylim=c(0,1),axes=F)
  plot(x,px,type="h",xlab="k",main=paste0("X~B(n=",input$nbin,"; p=",input$pbin,")"),ylab="f(k)",
       ylim=c(0,1),axes=F,lwd=5,col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  #rownames(bp)<-x
  bpm<-erw#*bp[rownames(bp)==floor(erw)]/floor(erw)
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.5,1))
  axis(1)
  abline(v=bpm,lty=2,col=2)
  text(bpm,0.9,
       paste0("E(X)=",round(input$nbin*input$pbin,4)),col=2)
  #arrows(bpm-0.05*input$nbin,0.5,bpm+0.05*input$nbin,0.5,lty=1,code=3,col=3)
  text(bpm+0.1*input$nbin,0.5,paste0("Var(X)=",round(input$nbin*input$pbin*(1-input$pbin),4)),col=3)
  
#geom
  input$pgeom = 0.6
  x<-seq(0,20,1)
  px<-dgeom(x,prob=input$pgeom)
  plot(x,px,xlab="k",type="h",main=paste0("X~G(p=",input$pgeom,")"),ylim=c(0,1),axes=F,ylab="f(k)",
       lwd=5,col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.5,1))
  axis(1)
  abline(v=1/input$pgeom,lty=2,col=2)
  par(xpd=T)
  text(1/input$pgeom,0.9,
       paste0("E(X)=",round(1/input$pgeom,4)),col=2)
  #arrows(1/input$pgeom-2,0.5,1/input$pgeom+2,0.5,lty=1,code=3,col=3)
  text(1/input$pgeom+3,0.5,paste0("Var(X)=",round((1-input$pgeom)/input$pgeom^2,4)),col=3)
  par(xpd=F)
  
  # pois
  input$lam=10
   x<-seq(0,2*input$lam,1)
  px<-dpois(x,input$lam)
  plot(x,px,xlab="k",type="h",main=substitute(paste("X~Poi(",lambda,"=",10,")"))
       ,ylab="f(k)",ylim=c(0,0.5),axes=F,lwd=5,col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.5))
  axis(1)
  abline(v=input$lam,lty=2,col=2)
  text(input$lam,0.49,
       paste0("E(X)=",round(input$lam,4)),col=2)
  #arrows(0.5*input$lam,0.4, 1.5*input$lam+2,0.4,lty=1, code=3,col=3)
  text(input$lam+3,0.4,paste0("Var(X)=",input$lam),col=3)
```


### Discrete uniform distribution

A discrete random variable $X$ is **uniformly distributed** on the value set $\mathcal T=\{x_1,x_2,\ldots, x_n\}$ if for all $i=1,\ldots, n$ the following holds:
\begin{equation*}
      f(x_i)=\frac{1}{n}.
\end{equation*}

- The following applies to such random variables:
\begin{align*}
      \mathbb E(X) &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n x_i\\
      \text{Var}(X) &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 - (\mathbb E(X))^2
\end{align*}

- Application examples:

    - Rolling a fair game dice
    - Selecting a customer from the pool of $n$ customers


```{r, echo=FALSE,out.width="100%"}
#htmltools::tags$iframe(title = "Diskrete_GV", src ="http://learning-dashboard.lehre.hwr-berlin.de:3838/Apps_RBook/#section-verteilungen",  width=760, height=400)
```



::: {.example #dgleichvert name="Product recommendations"}

To test how many product recommendations should be displayed to the customer during a shop visit in order to achieve the greatest effect, one could use discrete uniform distribution. (We want to recommend our products `r emoji::emoji("money_mouth_face")` but at the same time we don't want to annoy you too much or distract you from shopping `r emoji::emoji("exploding_head")`)

This would mean choosing a number between a minimum number of product recommendations per shop visit (e.g. only one recommendation) and a maximum (e.g. five product recommendations) for each customer with equal probability and then displaying this number of recommendations. You can then analyze what number of product recommendations would be optimal.

So in the background there is a uniform distribution and $X=$ 'The number of product recommendations displayed' is discretely equally distributed between $x_{min}=1$ and $x_{max}=5$.

- Set of values: $\{x_1=1;x_2=2;\ldots;x_5=5\}$
- Parameters: $n=5$
- Probability function: $f(x_i) = \frac 15$
- Parameters:

    - Expected value: $\mathbb E(X) = \frac 15(1 + 2 + \ldots +5) = 3$. On average, around 3 product recommendations are displayed.
    - Variance: $\text{Var}(X)=\frac 15 (1^2 + 2^2 + \ldots + 5^2) - 3^2 = 2$. So the standard deviation of the NUMBER of product recommendations is $\sqrt{2}$.
    
```{r echo=F, fig.cap="Discrete uniform distribution with n=5",width=320, height=300}
n=5
  x<-seq(1,n)
  px<-rep(1/n,n)
  plot(x,px,type="h",xlab="x-values",ylab="f(x)",main=paste0("X~disc.Uniform(n=",n,")"),
       ylim=c(0,0.4),axes=F,lwd=5,col=scales::alpha("gray44",0.5))
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4))
  axis(1,at=1:5, labels=1:5)
  
```


:::

***

### Bernoulli distribution

Let $A\in\mathcal F$ be an event with $p=\mathbb P(A)$.
The random variable $X=1_A$ indicating whether an event $A$ occurs or not (the indicator of $A$):
\begin{equation*}
    1_A=
      \begin{cases}
        1,&\text{ if $A$ occurs},\\
        0,&\text{ if $A$ does not occur}.
      \end{cases}
\end{equation*}
is **Bernoulli distributed** with parameter $p$ ($X\sim Ber(p)$).

The indicator function $1_A$ takes the value $1$ if event $A$ occurs and the value $0$ if $A$ does not occur.

Examples of $A$:

- an even number is obtained when rolling the dice;
- a loan customer does not repay his loan;
- a patient is cured by a specific therapy;
- the product of a delivery of goods is defective.

A random variable $X$ is **Bernoulli distributed** with parameter $p\in (0,1)$ if:

- Set of values: $\mathcal T=\{0,1\}$
- Probability function: $f(1)=p$, $f(0)=1-p$

Parameters:

- Expected value:
\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb E(X)=p.
\end{equation*}

- Variance:
\begin{equation*}
      \text{Var}(X)=p(1-p).
\end{equation*}

You can easily check:
\begin{align*}
      \mathbb E(X) &= 1\cdot f(1) + 0\cdot f(0) = p\\[3pt]
      \text{Var}(X) &= 1^2\cdot f(1) + 0^2\cdot f(0) - (\mathbb E(X))^2 %
                      = p - p^2 = p(1-p).
\end{align*}

***

::: {.example #dbernvert name="Indicator of app usage"}

In example \@ref(exm:ziehungbestKov), we defined the random variable $A=$"app usage indicator":

$A=\begin{cases}1, &\text{ if } App\\0, &\text{ if } Web.\end{cases}$

$A$ only takes two values $\{0;1\}$ and based on the occurrence of the event $App=$"a randomly selected customer uses the app". The probability $\mathbb P(App)=0.6$. This means:

- $A\sim Ber(p=0.6)$
- $\mathbb E(A) = 0.6$ and
- $\text{Var}(A) = 0.6\cdot (1-0.6) = 0.24.$

We took a little longer when we had to calculate these metrics "from scratch" in \@ref(exm:ziehungbestKov) `r emoji::emoji("smirking_face")`

```{r echo=F, fig.cap="Bernoulli distribution with p=0.6",width=320, height=300}
input<-list()
input$pber<-0.6
 x<-c(0,1)
  px<-c(1-input$pber,input$pber)
  erw<-input$pber
  var<-input$pber*(1-input$pber)
  plot(x,px,type="h",xlab="k",ylab = "f(k)",main=paste0("X~Ber(p=",input$pber,")"),ylim=c(0,1),xlim=c(-0.5,1.5),axes=F,lwd=5,col=alpha("gray44",0.3))
  #rownames(bp)<-x
  bpm<-erw#*bp[rownames(bp)==floor(erw)]/floor(erw)
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.5,1))
  axis(1,at=c(0,1))
  abline(v=erw,lty=2,col=2)
  text(erw,0.9,
       paste0("E(X)=",round(erw,4)),col=2)
  arrows(0.1,0.5,
         0.9,0.5,lty=1,
         code=3,col=3, length=0.15,angle=15)
  text(erw,0.6,paste0("Var(X)=",round(var,4)),col=3)
```


:::

***


### Binomial distribution

The binomial distribution results when the sum of hits is formed from $n$ independent repetitions of a Bernoulli experiment with constant probability $p$.

I.e., if a Bernoulli random process is repeated $n$ times with constant probability $p=\mathbb P(A)$, then
is the random variable
\begin{equation*}
      X=\text{"Number of attempts in which $A$ occurs''}
\end{equation*}
binomially distributed with parameters $n$ and $p$.

A random variable is called **binomially distributed** with the parameters $n$ and $p$, for short $X\sim B(n,p)$, if the following applies:
\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb P(X=k) = f(k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k\,
      (1-p)^{n-k},\quad\text{ for } k=0,1,\ldots, n,
\end{equation*}
and $f(k)=0$ otherwise.Remember: Binomial coefficient: $\displaystyle\binom{n}{k} = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$ with $n!=1\cdot 2\cdot \ldots \cdot n$ and $0!=1$.

- Parameters:
\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb E(X) = np\quad\text{ and }\quad \text{Var}(X) = n p(1-p)
\end{equation*}

- **Additive property**: The sum of independent binomial distributed random variables with the same parameter $p$ is again
    binomially distributed:

if $X\sim B(n,p)$ and $Y\sim B(m,p)$ are independent, then $X+Y\sim B(n+m,p)$.

- Application examples:

    - Number of loans in a portfolio that are not being serviced (assumption: loans default independently of each other.)
    - Number of patients cured by therapy;
    - Number of products in a delivery that are defective.


::: {.example #dbinvert name="Number of app users"}

If we select $n=10$ customers randomly (with replacement) from the customer pool and state whether they are app users or not, then we repeat the Bernoulli process: 'Drawing a random customer and determining the $App$-property' independently ten times.

The **probability of success**, here the probability of selecting an app user, corresponds to $\mathbb P(App)=0.6$ the proportion of app users in the entire customer pool.

So $X=$'The number of app users among the randomly selected ten customers' is binomially distributed:
$X\sim B(\color{green}{n=10}; \color{blue}{p=0.6})$

- We can now calculate the probabilities `r emoji::emoji("smiling_face_with_sunglasses")`:
\[\begin{align}
\mathbb P(X=\color{red}{5}) &=\binom{\color{green}{10}}{\color{red}{5}} \color{blue}{0.6}^\color{red}{5}\,(1-\color{blue}{0.6})^{\color{green}{10}-\color{red}{5}} = `r round(dbinom(5,10,0.6),4)`,\\
\mathbb P(X<2) &=\mathbb P(X=0) + \mathbb P(X=1)=\binom{\color{green}{10}}{0} \color{blue}{0.6}^0\cdot(1-\color{blue}{0.6}) ^{{\color{green}{10}}-0} + \binom{\color{green}{10}}{1} \color{blue}{0.6}^1\,(1-\color{blue}{0.6})^{\color{green}{10}-1} \\
&= `r round(dbinom(0,10,0.6)+dbinom(1,10,0.6),4)`,\\
\mathbb P(X=8) &=\binom{\color{green}{10}}{8} \color{blue}{0.6}^8\,(1-\color{blue}{0.6})^{\color{green}{10}-8} = `r round(dbinom(8,10,0.6),4)`.
\end{align}
\]

- Parameters:
\begin{align}
\mathbb E(X) &= n\cdot \color{blue}{p} = 10\cdot \color{blue}{0.6} = 6\\
\text{Var}(X)&= n\cdot \color{blue}{p}\cdot(1-\color{blue}{p}) = 10\cdot \color{blue}{0.6}\cdot (1-\color{blue}{0.6}) = 2.4.
\end{align}

```{r echo=F, fig.cap="Binomial distribution with n=10 and p=0.6",width=320, height=300}
 input$nbin=10
  input$pbin=0.6
   x<-seq(0,input$nbin,1)
  px<-dbinom(x,size=input$nbin,prob=input$pbin)
  erw<-input$nbin*input$pbin
  #bp<-barplot(px,names.arg=x,xlab="k",main=paste0("X~B(n=",input$nbin,"; p=",input$pbin,")"),ylim=c(0,1),axes=F)
  plot(x,px,type="h",xlab="k",main=paste0("X~B(n=",input$nbin,"; p=",input$pbin,")"),ylab="f(k)",
       ylim=c(0,1),axes=F,lwd=5,col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  #rownames(bp)<-x
  bpm<-erw#*bp[rownames(bp)==floor(erw)]/floor(erw)
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.5,1))
  axis(1)
  abline(v=bpm,lty=2,col=2)
  text(bpm,0.9,
       paste0("E(X)=",round(input$nbin*input$pbin,4)),col=2)
  #arrows(bpm-0.05*input$nbin,0.5,bpm+0.05*input$nbin,0.5,lty=1,code=3,col=3)
  text(bpm+0.1*input$nbin,0.5,paste0("Var(X)=",round(input$nbin*input$pbin*(1-input$pbin),4)),col=3)
```


:::

::: {.example #dbinvertKredit name="Loan portfolio"}

Consider a homogeneous loan portfolio with $500$ loans and default probability of $1$%.

- Random variable $A = \text{'default of a loan'}$ with values $a\in \{0 (\text{no default}),1 (\text{default})\}$ is Bernoulli distributed with $p=0.01$.
- Random variable $P = \text{'Number of defaults in the loan portfolio'}$ (assumptions: defaults occur independently of one another).
- Under the assumptions, $P$ is $\text{binomially distributed}$ with $\color{blue}{p=0.01}$ and $\color{green}{n=500}$: $P\sim B(500,0.01)$.

\begin{align*}
		\mathbb E(P) &= \color{green}{n}\color{blue}{p} = \color{green}{500}\cdot \color{blue}{0.01} = 5,\\
		\text{Var}(P) &=\color{green}{n}\color{blue}{p}(1-\color{blue}{p})=\color{green}{500}\cdot \color{blue}{0.01}\cdot (1-\color{blue}{0.01}) = 4.95,\\
		\mathbb P(P>1) &=1-\mathbb P(P\leq 1) =1-\sum_{k=0}^1\binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k} \\
		&=1- \Big(\frac{500!}{0!500!}(\color{blue}{0,01})^0(1-\color{blue}{0.01})^{500} + \frac{500!}{1!499!}(\color{blue}{0.01})^1(1-\color{blue}{0.01})^{499}\Big)=`r gsub("\\.","\\,",round(1-pbinom(1,500,0.01),4))`.
\end{align*}

```{r echo=F, fig.cap="Binomial distribution with n=500 and p=0.01",width=320, height=300}
 input$nbin=500
  input$pbin=0.01
   x<-seq(0,input$nbin,1)
  px<-dbinom(x,size=input$nbin,prob=input$pbin)
  erw<-input$nbin*input$pbin
  #bp<-barplot(px,names.arg=x,xlab="k",main=paste0("X~B(n=",input$nbin,"; p=",input$pbin,")"),ylim=c(0,1),axes=F)
  plot(x[x<=70],px[x<=70],type="h",xlab="k",main=paste0("X~B(n=",input$nbin,"; p=",input$pbin,")"),ylab="f(k)",
       ylim=c(0,1),axes=F,lwd=5,col=alpha("gray44",0.5),xlim=c(0,70))
  #rownames(bp)<-x
  bpm<-erw#*bp[rownames(bp)==floor(erw)]/floor(erw)
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.5,1))
  axis(1, at=c(seq(0,50,10),60,70), labels=c(seq(0,50,10),"...",500))
  abline(v=bpm,lty=2,col=2)
  text(bpm,0.9,
       paste0("E(X)=",round(input$nbin*input$pbin,4)),col=2)
  #arrows(bpm-0.05*input$nbin,0.5,bpm+0.05*input$nbin,0.5,lty=1,code=3,col=3)
  text(bpm+0.01*input$nbin,0.5,paste0("Var(X)=",round(input$nbin*input$pbin*(1-input$pbin),4)),col=3)
```
:::

***

::: {#q31 .quiz }

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text31"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer31" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj31)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result31"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj31 = generateQuestion_num(31);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text31').textContent = "An insurance company has insurance contracts with \\(100\\) independent corporate customers who have a probability of \\(" + questionObj31.question[0] + "\\%\\) of claiming compensation for some damage. How likely is it that not a single corporate customer will apply for compensation? (Round to four decimal places).\\[\\]\\(X= \\) 'Number of compensation payments'  follows a Binomial distribution with \\(n=\\)";
    questionObj31.answer = 100
```

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text32"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer32" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj32)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result32"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj32 = generateQuestion_num(32);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text32').textContent = "and \\(p=\\)";
    questionObj32.answer = questionObj31.question[0]/100
```

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text33"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer33" placeholder="Enter your answer">\\()\\)
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj33)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result33"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj33 = generateQuestion_num(33);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text33').textContent = "The desired probability \\(\\mathbb P(X=\\)";
    questionObj33.answer = 0
    
```

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text34"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer34" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj34)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result34"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj34 = generateQuestion_num(34);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text34').textContent = "is therefore:";
    questionObj34.answer = binomialCDF(0,100,questionObj31.question[0]/100)
```

---

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text35"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer35" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj35)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result35"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj35 = generateQuestion_num(35);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text35').textContent = "A statistics test consists of \\(" + (questionObj35.question[0]*10) + "\\) multiple-choice questions, where each question has four possible answers and only one answer is correct. What is the probability of getting at least \\(" + Math.floor(questionObj35.question[0]*8) + "\\) points if you try to guess the answers? (round to four decimal places).\\[\\]\\(X= \\) 'Number of questions answered correctly'  follows a Binomial distribution with \\(n=\\)";
    questionObj35.answer = (questionObj35.question[0]*10)
```

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text36"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer36" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj36)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result36"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj36 = generateQuestion_num(36);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text36').textContent = "and \\(p=\\)";
    questionObj36.answer = 0.25
```

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text37"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer37" placeholder="Enter your answer">\\()\\)
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj37)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result37"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj37 = generateQuestion_num(37);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text37').textContent = "The desired probability \\(\\mathbb P(X\\geq \\)";
    questionObj37.answer = Math.floor(questionObj35.question[0]*8)
    
```

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text38"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer38" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj38)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result38"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj38 = generateQuestion_num(38);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text38').textContent = "is therefore:";
    questionObj38.answer = binomialCDF(Math.floor(questionObj35.question[0]*8),(questionObj35.question[0]*10),0.25)
```

:::

<!--::: {.exercise name="Discrete distributions"}

```{r, echo=FALSE,out.width="100%"}
htmltools::tags$iframe(title = "quiz_dv", src ="./htmls/quiz_dv.html",  width=760, height=500)
```

:::-->



***

### Geometric distribution `r #emoji::emoji("alien")`

If a Bernoulli experiment is repeated with the same probability $p=\mathbb P(A)$ until the first one
The random variable is the time the event of interest $A$ occurs
\begin{equation*}
      X=\text{"Number of attempts until $A$ occurs for the first time''}
\end{equation*}
is **geometrically distributed** with parameter $p$.

Application examples:

- Duration (“discrete waiting time” in days) until the first failure of one
      device,
- Duration in days until an insurance customer makes one for the first time
      reports damage,
- Number of customers interviewed (waiting time in customers `r emoji::emoji("smile")`), until the first time a customer with the desired characteristic is interviewed.
      
A random variable $X$ is said to be **geometrically distributed** ($X\sim G(p)$) with parameter $p$ if:
\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb P(X=k)=f(k) = (1-p)^{k-1}\cdot p, \quad \text{ for }
      k=1,\ldots,
\end{equation*}
and $f(k)=0$ else.

- Parameters:
\begin{align*}
       \mathbb E(X) &= \sum _{k=1}^\infty k\, p\, (1-p)^{k-1} = \frac{1}{p}\\
       \text{Var}(X) &= \sum_{k=1}^\infty k^2 \, p\, (1-p)^{k-1} -
              \frac{1}{p^2} = \frac{1-p}{p^2}
\end{align*}



***

::: {.example #dgeomvert name="App user in a customer Survey"}

A survey of $n=50$ customers is conducted. Customers are randomly selected in the survey and can take part multiple times. The proportion of customers who use the app is 60%.

- Random variable: $M = \text{'Number of app users in the customer survey'}$, $M\sim B(n=50,p=0.6)$.

- Random variable $V = \text{'Number of respondents until an app user is interviewed'}$ is geometrically distributed $V\sim G(p=0.6)$.
\begin{align*}
\mathbb E(V) &= \frac 1p = 1.6667,\\
\text{Var}(V) &=\frac{1-p}{p^2}=\frac{0.4}{0.36} = 1.1111,\\
\mathbb P(V< 5)& = \mathbb P(V\leq 4) =f_{G_{0.6}}(1) + f_{G_{0.6}}(2) + \ldots + f_ {G_{0,6}}(4),\\
&=(1-0.6)^0\cdot 0.6 + (1-0.6)^1\cdot 0.6 + \ldots + (1-0.6)^3\cdot 0.6 = `r gsub("\\.","\\,",round(pgeom(4,0.6),4))`.
\end{align*}

```{r echo=F, fig.cap="Geometric distribution with p=0.6",width=320, height=300}
  input$pgeom = 0.6
  x<-seq(0,20,1)
  px<-dgeom(x,prob=input$pgeom)
  plot(x,px,xlab="k",type="h",main=paste0("X~G(p=",input$pgeom,")"),ylim=c(0,1),axes=F,ylab="f(k)",
       lwd=5,col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.5,1))
  axis(1)
  abline(v=1/input$pgeom,lty=2,col=2)
  par(xpd=T)
  text(1/input$pgeom+1,0.9,
       paste0("E(X)=",round(1/input$pgeom,4)),col=2)
  #arrows(1/input$pgeom-2,0.5,1/input$pgeom+2,0.5,lty=1,code=3,col=3)
  text(1/input$pgeom+3,0.5,paste0("Var(X)=",round((1-input$pgeom)/input$pgeom^2,4)),col=3)
  par(xpd=F)
```

:::

***

### Poisson distribution `r #emoji::emoji("alien")`

Sometimes we also need a distribution that indicates the number of "successes" **per unit of time**. For example, if the random process "continues", such as an online shop is always available and the number of customers who log in per hour is of interest.

We may be interested in the following random variable:
\begin{equation*}
      X=\text{"Number of events that occur within a time interval $[0,1]$''}.
\end{equation*}

Under the following assumptions, $X$ is **Poisson distributed**:

- Two events cannot occur at the same time.
- The probability that an event occurs in a small time interval of length $\Delta t$ is approximately $\lambda\Delta t$.
- The probability of occurrence depends only on the length of the time interval, not on the location.
- The numbers of events in disjoint subintervals are independent.

A random variable $X$ is **Poisson distributed** ($X\sim Poi(\lambda)$) with parameter $\lambda$ if:
\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb P(X=k) = f(k) = \frac{\lambda^k}{k!}\, \text{e}^{-\lambda}, \quad
      k=0,1,2,\ldots,
\end{equation*}
and $f(k)=0$ else.

The parameter $\lambda$ is also called **intensity rate**.

- Parameters:
\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb E(X)=\lambda\quad \text{ and } \quad \text{Var}(X)=\lambda.
\end{equation*}

- **Addition property**:
If $X\sim Poi(\lambda)$ and $Y\sim Poi(\mu)$ are independent, then:
\begin{equation*}
    X+Y\sim Poi(\lambda+\mu).
\end{equation*}

In particular, it follows that the number of events in an interval of length $t$ is Poisson distributed with parameters
    $\lambda t$.

- Application examples:

    - the number of loan defaults by a bank in a given accounting period;
    - the number of customers arriving at a counter during a certain period of time;
   

***

::: {.example #dpoisvert name="Advertisement"}

An advertising banner will be displayed on your website between 8 a.m. and 10 a.m. or 7 p.m. and 8 p.m. The visitors to your website act independently and the click rate ($\lambda$) in the two time periods is constant and is 5 thousand clicks per hour.

- $B_{morning} = \text{'Number of clicks between 8 and 10 a.m.'}$, $B_M\sim Poi(\lambda_{morning}=2\cdot\lambda=10)$.

- $B_{evening} = \text{'Number of clicks between 7 p.m. and 8 p.m.'}$, $B_A\sim Poi(\lambda_{evening}=\lambda=5)$.

- Because of the addition property of the Poisson distribution: $B_{total} = \text{'number of clicks on your advertising banner in the two time periods'}$ is also Poisson distributed $B_{total}\sim Poi(\lambda_{total} =\lambda _{morning}+\lambda _{evening}=15)$.
\[\begin{align}
\mathbb E(B_{total}) &= \lambda_{total} = 15,\\
\text{Var}(B_{total}) &=\lambda_{total}=15,\\
\mathbb P(B_{total}>10)&=1-\mathbb P(B_{total}\leq 10) = 1-(f_{poi_{15}}(0) + f_{poi_{15}}( 1) + \ldots+ f_{poi_{15}}(10))\\ &=1-\exp(-15)\cdot\Big(\frac{15^0}{0!}+\frac{15^ 1}{1!}+\ldots+\frac{15^{10}}{10!}\Big)= `r round(1-ppois(10, 15),4)`.
\end{align}\]

```{r echo=F, fig.cap="Poisson distribution with \\(\\lambda=15\\)",width=320, height=300}
 input$lam=15
   x<-seq(0,2*input$lam,1)
  px<-dpois(x,input$lam)
  plot(x,px,xlab="k",type="h",main=substitute(paste("X~Poi(",lambda,"=",15,")"))
       ,ylab="f(k)",ylim=c(0,0.5),axes=F,lwd=5,col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  axis(2, at=c(0,0.25,0.5))
  axis(1)
  abline(v=input$lam,lty=2,col=2)
  text(input$lam,0.49,
       paste0("E(X)=",round(input$lam,4)),col=2)
  #arrows(0.5*input$lam,0.4, 1.5*input$lam+2,0.4,lty=1, code=3,col=3)
  text(input$lam+3,0.4,paste0("Var(X)=",input$lam),col=3)
```

:::

***

<!--
#### Poissonverteilung approximiert Binomialverteilung{-}

Unter bestimmten Voraussetzungen ($n\gg 1$ und $p\ll 1$, d.h.$n$ groß und $p$ klein) kann die Binomialverteilung durch die Poisson-verteilung mit $\lambda=n\, p$ approximieren (annähern). Dies erleichtert etwas die Berechnungen (die Wahrscheinlichkeitsfunktion der Poisson-Verteilung ist etwas einfacher zu berechnen).

Approximation & Binomialverteilung $B(n,p)$ $\approx$ Poissonverteilung $Poi(\lambda)$:

\begin{equation*}
      f_B(k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k} \approx f_P(k) =
      \text e^{-\lambda}\, \frac{\lambda^k}{k!}
\end{equation*}

Also, wenn $X$: Anzahl des Eintretens eines zufälligen Ereignisses $A$ mit kleiner Wahrscheinlichkeit $\mathbb P(A)$ in einer langen Serie unabhängiger Versuche darstellt, ist $X$ is approximativ Poisson-verteilt. Poisson-Verteilung ist in diesem Fall als die **Verteilung der seltenen Ereignisse** zu interpretieren.

::: {.example #dbinpoisaprox name="Kreditportfolio approximiert"}

Fortführung von \@ref(exm:dbinvertKredit)

Betrachte ein homogenes Kreditportfolio mit $500$ Krediten und Ausfallwahrscheinlichkeit von $1$%.

$P = \text{'Anzahl der Ausfälle im Kreditportfolio'}$, $P\sim B(n=500,p=0,01)$.
$\lambda=n\, p= 500\cdot 0,01 = 5$ und $P\stackrel{approx.}{\sim}{} Poi(5).$

$$
\begin{array}{rcc}
  \hline 
  k & f_B(k) & f_{Poi}(k)\\
  \hline
  0 & 0,0066 & 0,0067\\
  1 & 0,0332 & 0,0337\\
  2 & 0,0836 & 0,0842\\
  3 & 0,1402 & 0,1404\\
  4 & 0,1760 & 0,1755\\
  5 & 0,1764 & 0,1755\\\hline
\end{array}
$$

```{r,echo=F,fig.cap='Wktsfunktion der Binomialverteilung und der Poisson-Apprximation', out.width='80%', fig.asp=.75, fig.align='center'}
# n=500; p=0.01
# lam=n*p
# 
# x<-seq(0,20,1)
# ppx<-dpois(x,lam)
# pbx<-dbinom(x,n,p)
# 
# plot(x,pbx,xlab="k",type="h",ylab="f(k)",ylim=c(0,0.2),axes=F,lwd=2,col=alpha(3,0.5))
# points(x,pbx,pch=15,col=alpha(3,0.5))
# lines(x+0.2,ppx,type="h",col=alpha(2,0.5),lwd=2)
# points(x+0.2,ppx,pch=15,col=alpha(2,0.5))
# axis(2, at=c(0,0.1,0.2))
# axis(1)
# legend("topright",bty="n",col=c(alpha(3,0.5),alpha(2,0.5)),lwd=c(3,3),legend=c("B(500;0,01)", "Poi(5)"))
# #abline(v=input$lam,lty=2,col=2)
# #text(input$lam,0.49,paste0("E(X)=",round(input$lam,4)),col=2)
# #arrows(0.5*input$lam,0.4, 1.5*input$lam+2,0.4,lty=1, code=3,col=3)
# #text(input$lam+3,0.4,paste0("Var(X)=",input$lam),col=3)
```


:::

***
-->

***

::: {#q32 .quiz }

```{js, echo=F}


// Generate a random question
  const questionObj39 = {
      question: "\\(25\\%\\) of BVG buses on your route have working air conditioning. On a hot day, you will only ride if there is air conditioning to cool the bus. So you usually wait for an air-conditioned bus to arrive. Which distribution is relevant for the random variable \\(X =\\) 'Number of buses that you don't ride on until an air-conditioned bus finally pulls up'?",
      options: ['\\(Ber(0.25)\\)', '\\(B(n=100,p=0.25)\\)', ' \\(G(p=0.25)\\)', '\\(Poi(\\lambda=0.25)\\)'],
      answer: '3',
      id: 39
    };
```

<p class="question"> <span id="question-text39"></span></p>
<div class="options">
  <label>
      <input type="radio" name="option39" value=1> \\(Ber(0.25)\\)
    </label>
    <label>
  <input type="radio" name="option39" value=2> \\(B(n=100,p=0.25)\\)
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="radio" name="option39" value=3> \\(G(p=0.25)\\)
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="radio" name="option39" value=4> \\(Poi(\\lambda=0.25)\\)
    </label>
  </div>
  <button onclick="checkAnswer_match(questionObj39)">Submit</button>
  <p id="result39"></p>

```{js, echo=F}
    // Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text39').textContent = questionObj39.question;

```

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text310"></span></p>
<input type="number" id="answer310" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj310)">Submit</button>
<p id="result310"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj310 = generateQuestion_num(310);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text310').textContent = "On average, how many buses have to pull up before you get on?";
    questionObj310.answer = 4
```

```{js, echo=F}
  // Generate a random question
    const questionObj311 = {
      question: 'Five buses have already left without air conditioning. So you give up and go to the S-Bahn - at least you can open the windows there. S-Bahn traffic is once again irregular due to a police operation. On average, \\(2\\) S-Bahn trains run every hour at irregular intervals. If \\(Y = \\)"The number of S-Bahn trains that will run in the next \\(15\\) minutes" is modeled, then:',
      options: ['\\(Ber(0.5)\\)', '\\(B(n=4,p=0.5)\\)', ' \\(G(p=0.5)\\)', '\\(Poi(\\lambda=0.5)\\)'],
      answer: '4',
      id: 311
    };
```

<p class="question"> <span id="question-text311"></span></p>
<div class="options">
  <label>
      <input type="radio" name="option311" value=1> \\(Ber(0.5)\\)
    </label>
    <label>
  <input type="radio" name="option311" value=2> \\(B(n=4,p=0.5)\\)
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="radio" name="option311" value=3> \\(G(p=0.5)\\)
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="radio" name="option311" value=4> \\(Poi(\\lambda=0.5)\\)
    </label>
   </div>
  <button onclick="checkAnswer_match(questionObj311)">Submit</button>
  <p id="result311"></p>

```{js, echo=F}
  // Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text311').textContent = questionObj311.question;
```
 
<p id="question"> <span id="question-text312"></span></p>
<input type="number" id="answer312" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj312)">Submit</button>
<p id="result312"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj312 = generateQuestion_num(312);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text312').textContent = "The probability that no S-Bahn will come in the next 15 minutes is:";
    questionObj312.answer = Math.pow(0.5,0)/factorial(0)*Math.pow(Math.E,-0.5)
```


:::

<!--::: {.exercise name="Spezielle diskrete Verteilungen"}

```{r, echo=FALSE,out.width="100%"}
htmltools::tags$iframe(title = "zusatz_dv", src ="./htmls/zusatz_dv.html",  width=760, height=500)
```

:::-->

***

## Continuous distributions

There are also continuous random variables distribution models that are used in several contexts. We will learn about a pair of continuous distributions here.

```{r, echo=F, fig.cap="Distributional models for continuous random variables",width=640, height=600}
#norm
par(mfrow=c(2,4), mar=c(1.5,2.5,3,1))
mu<-input$mun<-20; sig<-input$varn<-4
  xseq<-seq(mu-4*sig,max(mu+4*sig,0.2),length=1000)
  dseq<-dnorm(xseq,mu,sig)
  plot(xseq, dseq,
       type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=range(xseq),
       ylim=c(0,0.5)
       ,ylab="",xlab="",main=expression(phi(x)))
  lines(xseq,dnorm(xseq,0,1),col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  arrows(mu,0,mu,max(dseq),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  arrows(mu,max(dseq),min(xseq),max(dseq),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(floor(min(xseq)),mu,0,ceiling(max(xseq))),labels=c("",paste(mu),"",""))
  axis(2,at=c(0,round(dnorm(mu,mu,sig),2),0.5),labels=c(0,"",0.5),las=2)
  text(10,round(dnorm(mu,mu,sig),2)+0.1,paste0("f(x=",mu,")=",round(dnorm(mu,mu,sig),3)),col=4)
  par(xpd=T)
  if(mu!=0){text(0,-0.2,"0",col="gray44")}
  #legend("topright",col="gray44",lty=1,legend="N(0,1)",border="n")
  
  plot(xseq, pnorm(xseq,mu,sig),
       type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=range(xseq),
       ylim=c(0,1.1),ylab="",xlab="",main="N(x)")
  arrows(min(xseq),1,max(xseq),1,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  #arrows(min(xseq),0.5,mu,0.5,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(min(xseq),mu, 0,max(xseq)),labels=c("","","",""))
  axis(2,at=c(0,0.5,1),labels=c(0,0.5,1),las=2)
  text(mu,0.4,paste("E(X)=",mu),col=2)
  text(mu+0.2,0.2,paste("Var(X)=",round(sig^2,2)),col=3)
  lines(xseq,pnorm(xseq,0,1),col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  if(mu!=0){text(0,-0.1,"0",col="gray44")}
  
  plot(1,1,col=NA,axes=F,ylab="",xlab="")
   plot(1,1,col=NA,axes=F,ylab="",xlab="")
  
  #exp
  lam<-0.1
  xseq<-seq(0,100,0.01)
  plot(xseq, dexp(xseq,lam),
       type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(0,max(xseq)),
       ylim=c(0,0.5),ylab="",xlab="",main=expression(paste("E(",lambda,"=",0.1,")","  f(x)")))
  #lines(xseq,dexp(xseq,1),col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  arrows(1/lam,0,1/lam,dexp(1/lam,lam),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  arrows(1/lam,dexp(1/lam,lam),min(xseq),dexp(1/lam,lam),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(floor(min(xseq)),1/lam,0,ceiling(max(xseq))),labels=c("",paste("E(X)=",1/lam),"",""),cex=0.5)
  axis(2,at=c(0,round(dexp(lam,lam),2),0.5),
       labels=c(0,"",0.5),las=2)
  par(xpd=T)
  if(lam!=1){text(0,-0.2,"0",col="gray44")}
  text(22,0.1,paste0("f(x=",lam,")=",round(dexp(lam,lam),2)), col=4)
  #legend("topright",col="gray44",lty=1,legend="E(1)",border="n")
  
  plot(xseq, pexp(xseq,lam),
       type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(0,max(xseq)),
       ylim=c(0,1.1),ylab="",xlab="",main=expression(paste("E(",lambda,"=",0.1,")","  F(x)")))
  arrows(0,1,max(xseq),1,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  #arrows(min(xseq),0.5,mu,0.5,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(0,1/lam, 0,max(xseq)),labels=c(0,"","",""))
  axis(2,at=c(0,0.5,1),labels=c(0,0.5,1),las=2)
  text(1/lam+5,0.4,paste("E(X)=",round(1/lam,2)),col=2)
  text(1/lam+5,0.2,paste("Var(X)=",round(1/lam^2,2)),col=3)
  #lines(xseq,pexp(xseq,1),col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  
  #unif
  input$astet<-0
  input$bstet<-20
  plot(c(input$astet,input$bstet), 
       rep(1/(input$bstet -input$astet),2),type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(input$astet-0.1*input$bstet,input$bstet+0.1*input$bstet),
       ylim=c(0,max(0.5,1/(input$bstet - input$astet)+0.1)),ylab="",xlab="",main="U(a,b)  f(x)")
  arrows(input$astet,0,input$astet,1/(input$bstet - input$astet),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  arrows(input$bstet,0,input$bstet,1/(input$bstet - input$astet),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(input$astet,input$bstet),labels=paste(c("a=","b="),c(input$astet,input$bstet)))
  axis(2,at=c(0,1/(input$bstet - input$astet),0.5),labels=c(0,paste0("1/(b-a)=",round(1/(input$bstet - input$astet),2)),0.5),las=2)
  
  plot(c(input$astet-0.1*input$bstet,input$astet,input$bstet,input$bstet+0.1*input$bstet), 
       c(0,0,1,1),type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(input$astet-0.1*input$bstet,input$bstet+0.1*input$bstet),
       ylim=c(0,1.1),ylab="",xlab="",main="U(a,b)   F(x)")
  arrows(input$bstet,0,input$bstet,1,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  arrows(input$astet-0.1*input$bstet,1,input$bstet,1,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(input$astet,round((input$astet+input$bstet)/2,2),input$bstet),labels=paste(c("a=","","b="),c(input$astet,"",input$bstet)))
  axis(2,at=c(0,0.5,1),labels=c(0,0.5,1),las=2)
  par(xpd=T)
  text((input$astet+input$bstet)/2,0.4,paste("E(X)=",round((input$astet+input$bstet)/2,2)),col=2)
  text((input$astet+input$bstet)/2,0.2,paste("Var(X)=",round((input$bstet-input$astet)^2/12,2)),col=3)
  
  #chi2
  

```

```{r,echo=F}
#knitr::include_url(paste0(link,"Apps_RBook/#section-verteilungen"),height = "600px")
```

### Normal distribution

The **normal distribution** is the best known and most important distribution. In many applications, the empirical distribution of a feature can be approximated sufficiently well using a normal distribution.

In particular, the normal distribution is a good model if the underlying variable results from the additive overlap of a large number of independent, random individual effects. The theoretical justification for this empirically observable phenomenon is provided by the **Central Limit Theorem** ($\leadsto$ next chapter)

The normal distribution is often called the **Gauss distribution**, named after the mathematician Carl Friedrich Gauß (1777--1855).

A random variable $X$ is said to be **normally distributed** with parameters $\mu\in \mathbb R$ and $\sigma>0$, for short $X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)$, if it has the has the following density:
\begin{equation*}
      \varphi(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
      \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad x\in \mathbb R.
\end{equation*}

- Key figures:
\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb E(X)=\mu\quad\text{ and }\quad \text{Var}(X)=\sigma^2.
\end{equation*}

- With $\mu=0$ and $\sigma^2=1$ you get the **standard normal distribution** $N(0,1)$ with density
\begin{equation*}
      \varphi(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}
      \exp\left(-\frac{x^2}{2}\right), \quad x\in \mathbb R.
\end{equation*}

- The density of the standard normal distribution is denoted as $\varphi(x)$ and the distribution function as $N(x)$.

- Properties of normal distribution

    - The density is **symmetrical about $\mu$**, i.e.
    \begin{equation*}
        \varphi(\mu-x) = \varphi(\mu+x), \quad x\in \mathbb R.
    \end{equation*}
    In particular in the case $\mu=0$: $\varphi(-x)=\varphi(x)$.
    - The following applies to the distribution function of the standard normal distribution: $N(-x) = 1- N(x)$
    - **Standardize**:
    
        - $X$ is $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$-distributed $\iff$ $\displaystyle\frac{X-\mu}{\sigma}$ is $N(0,1)$-distributed .
        - $Y$ is $N(0,1)$-distributed $\iff$ $\mu + \sigma Y$ is $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$-distributed.

    - Probabilities within $\sigma$ bounds
    \begin{align*}
    \mathbb P(|X-\mu|\leq \sigma) &= 0.6827\\
    \mathbb P(|X-\mu|\leq 2\sigma) &= 0.9545\\
    \mathbb P(|X-\mu|\leq 3\sigma) &= 0.9973
    \end{align*}
    - Addition property: If $X\sim N(\mu_X,\sigma_X^2)$ and $Y\sim N(\mu_Y,\sigma_Y^2)$ are independent, then
    \begin{equation*}
    X+Y\sim
    N(\mu_X+\mu_Y, \sigma_X^2+\sigma_Y^2).
    \end{equation*}
    
- Probability with which an $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$-distributed random variable lies between the boundaries $a$ and $b$:

\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb P(a\leq X\leq b) = \mathbb P\left(\frac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\leq
        \frac{X-\mu}{\sigma} \leq \frac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\right)
      = N\left(\frac{b-\mu}{\sigma}\right) -
      N\left(\frac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\right)
\end{equation*}

***

::: {.example #normverttemp name="Average temperature"}

We model the average temperature in Berlin in July as a random variable $T: \text{'Average temperature in Berlin in July'}$ with values $t\in \mathbb R$.

- $T\stackrel{}{\sim}{} N(\mu_T,\sigma_T)$ with $\mu_T=\mathbb E(T)=20$, $\text{Var}(T)=16$ and $\sigma_T = 4$.


- Probability:
\begin{align*}
\mathbb P(|T-20|\leq 4) &\approx 0.68,\\
\mathbb P(|T-20|\leq 8) &\approx 0.95,\\
\mathbb P(15\leq T\leq 25)& = \mathbb P(T\leq 25)-\mathbb P(T<15) =N\Big(\frac{25-20}{4}\Big) - N\Big(\frac{15-20}{4}\Big)\\
& = `r round(pnorm(25,20,4),4)` - `r round(pnorm(15,20,4),4)` = `r round(pnorm(25,20,4) - pnorm(15,20,4 ),4)`.
\end{align*}

```{r, echo=F, fig.cap="Distributional models for continuous random variables",width=640, height=600}
#norm
par(mfrow=c(1,2))
mu<-input$mun<-20; sig<-input$varn<-4
  xseq<-seq(mu-4*sig,max(mu+4*sig,0.2),length=1000)
  dseq<-dnorm(xseq,mu,sig)
  plot(xseq, dseq,
       type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=range(xseq),
       ylim=c(0,0.5)
       ,ylab="",xlab="",main=expression(phi(x)))
  lines(xseq,dnorm(xseq,0,1),col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  arrows(mu,0,mu,max(dseq),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  arrows(mu,max(dseq),min(xseq),max(dseq),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(floor(min(xseq)),mu,0,ceiling(max(xseq))),labels=c("",paste(mu),"",""))
  axis(2,at=c(0,round(dnorm(mu,mu,sig),2),0.5),labels=c(0,"",0.5),las=2)
  text(10,round(dnorm(mu,mu,sig),2)+0.1,paste0("f(x=",mu,")=",round(dnorm(mu,mu,sig),3)),col=4)
  par(xpd=T)
  if(mu!=0){text(0.1,-0.05,"0",col="gray44")}
  #legend("topright",col="gray44",lty=1,legend="N(0,1)",border="n")
  
  plot(xseq, pnorm(xseq,mu,sig),
       type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=range(xseq),
       ylim=c(0,1.1),ylab="",xlab="",main="N(x)")
  arrows(min(xseq),1,max(xseq),1,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  #arrows(min(xseq),0.5,mu,0.5,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(min(xseq),mu, 0,max(xseq)),labels=c("","","",""))
  axis(2,at=c(0,0.5,1),labels=c(0,0.5,1),las=2)
  text(mu,0.4,paste("E(X)=",mu),col=2)
  text(mu+0.2,0.2,paste("Var(X)=",round(sig^2,2)),col=3)
  lines(xseq,pnorm(xseq,0,1),col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  if(mu!=0){text(0,-0.1,"0",col="gray44")}
  

```
:::

***

::: {.example #normvertmittel name="Sample mean of normally distributed random variables"}

If we make a random selection (with replacement) from a normally distributed population and write down a possible result as $n$ random variable "placeholders" as $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$, or calculate the mean $\bar X$ , then:

- $X_i\sim N(\mu_X,\sigma_X^2)$ by assumption, since we are selecting from a normally distributed population.

- $X_i$ independent, as you move with replacement

- $\bar X = \frac 1n\sum_{i=1}^n$ using the additive property of the normal distribution,

- $\bar X\sim N(\mu_X,\frac{\sigma_X^2}n)$, since $\mathbb E(\bar n X_i) = \frac 1n\cdot n\cdot \mu_X$ and $\text{Var}(\bar frac 1{n^2}\cdot n\cdot \sigma_X^2$.

- Or $\frac{\bar X-\mu_X}{\frac{\sigma_X}{\sqrt n}}\sim N(0;1)$.

:::

***

::: {.example #normvertlinreg name="Linear regression under the normal distribution assumption"}

Standard Single Linear Regression Model:

\begin{equation*}
      Y_i = \alpha + \beta\, x_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad i=1,\ldots, n.
\end{equation*}

These include:

- $Y_1,\ldots, Y_n$: observable metric random variable;
- $x_1,\ldots, x_n$: given deterministic values ​​(or
    Realizations of a ZV $X$);
- $\varepsilon_1, \ldots, \varepsilon_n$: unobservables
      ZV's that are independent and identically distributed with
      $\mathbb E(\varepsilon_i)=0$, $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i)=\sigma^2$.

Under the normal distribution assumption $\varepsilon_i\sim N(0,\sigma^2)\quad i=1,\ldots,n$ independent of $X_i\sim N(\mu_X,\sigma_X^2)$ (in case $X $ random) applies:

\begin{equation*}
      Y_i\sim N(\alpha+ \beta x_i,\sigma^2), \quad i=1,\ldots,n.
\end{equation*}

and

\begin{align*}
\hat\beta &= \frac{\sum _{i=1}^n (x_i-\overline x) (Y_i-\overline Y)} {\sum _{i=1}^n (x_i-\overline x) ^2}\\
&\sim N(\beta, \sigma_{\hat\beta}^2),\\
\hat\alpha &=\overline Y-\hat\beta \overline x\\
&\sim N(\alpha, \sigma_{\hat\alpha}^2).
\end{align*}

since these random variables represent a linear combination of independent, normally distributed random variables.
:::

::: {#q33 .quiz }




<p id="question"> <span id="question-text313"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer313" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj313)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result313"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj313 = generateQuestion_num(313);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text313').textContent = "If \\(X\\sim N(\\mu="+ questionObj310.question[0]+ ";\\sigma^2=" + Math.pow(questionObj312.question[0],2)+ ")\\) then \\(\\mathbb P(X>" + questionObj313.question[0] + ")=\\)";
    questionObj313.answer = 1-normalCDF(questionObj313.question[0],questionObj310.question[0],questionObj312.question[0])
```

---

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text314"></span></p>
<input type="number" id="answer314" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj314)">Submit</button>
<p id="result314"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj314 = generateQuestion_num(314);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text314').textContent = "\\(Z\\) is a standard normally distributed random variable. What is \\(c\\) such that: \\(\\mathbb P(-c\\leq Z\\leq c)=90\\%\\)?";
    questionObj314.answer = 1.644854
```

---

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text315"></span></p>
<input type="number" id="answer315" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj315)">Submit</button>
<p id="result315"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj315 = generateQuestion_num(315);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text315').textContent = "The weekly demand for an art object in an online art store is normally distributed with mean \\(50\\) and standard deviation \\(5\\). The store typically has an inventory of \\(60\\) pieces and orders once a week.\\[\\] What is the probability that demand will not be met in a week?";
    questionObj315.answer = 1-normalCDF(60,50,5)
```

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text316"></span></p>
<input type="number" id="answer316" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj316)">Submit</button>
<p id="result316"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj316 = generateQuestion_num(316);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text316').textContent = "How high should the inventory be so that a week's demand is not met only 10% of the time? (round to the next higher whole number)";
    questionObj316.answer = 57
```


```{js, echo=F}
  // Generate a random question
    const questionObj317 = {
      question: "Now we are interested in the random variable \\(Z=\\) 'Number of weeks in the year when the inventory is not sufficient' (hint: \\(52\\) weeks in the year). How is \\(Z\\) distributed?",
      options: ['\\(B(50,0.0228)\\)' ,'\\(B(52;0.0228)\\)','\\(N(50,25)\\)', '\\(N(52,25)\\)'],
      answer: '2',
      id: 317
    };
```

<p class="question"> <span id="question-text317"></span></p>
<div class="options">
  <label>
      <input type="radio" name="option317" value=1> \\(B(26,0.0228)\\)
    </label>
    <label>
  <input type="radio" name="option317" value=2> \\(B(52;0.0228)\\)
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="radio" name="option317" value=3> \\(N(26,25)\\)
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="radio" name="option317" value=4> \\(N(52,5)\\)
    </label>
    <label>
      <input type="radio" name="option317" value=5> \\(N(52,25)\\)
    </label>
  </div>
  <button onclick="checkAnswer_match(questionObj317)">Submit</button>
  <p id="result317"></p>

```{js, echo=F}
  // Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text317').textContent = questionObj317.question;
```
 


:::


***



### Exponential distribution `r #emoji::emoji("alien")`

The **exponential distribution** is often used to model waiting times under the assumption that the remaining waiting time does not depend on the lifespan that has already elapsed ("**memorylessness**").

Application examples:

- Lifespan of products or technical systems
- Processing time of customer orders
- Waiting time until the next call arrives in the call center

A continuous random variable $X$ is called **exponentially distributed** with parameter $\lambda>0$, for short $X\sim E(\lambda)$, if it has the density:
\begin{equation*}
      f(x) = \lambda\, \text e^{-\lambda x}, \quad \text{ for } x\geq 0,
\end{equation*}
and $f(x)=0$ else.

- The distribution function is
    $F(x)=1-\text e^{-\lambda x}$, for $x>0$.

- Key figures:
\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb E(X) = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad\text{ and }\quad
      \text{Var}(X)=\frac{1}{\lambda^2}.
\end{equation*}

***

::: {.example #dexptrans name="Transaction duration in payment service"}

```{r,echo=F}
erw=10
lam=1/erw
lamm<-gsub("\\.","\\,",round(lam,4))

dauer=5
```


As part of fraud prevention, you monitor transaction times (i.e. the waiting times until a payment transaction is completed) in seconds.

You model the random variable $X =$"transaction duration in seconds" as exponentially distributed. The average transaction duration is $5$ seconds.

- $\mathbb E(X) = `r erw`$ $\leadsto$ $\lambda = \frac 1{\mathbb E(X)}=\frac 1{`r erw`}=`r lamm`$

- $X\sim E(`r lamm`)$ and

- the probability of transaction times less than `r dauer` seconds: 

$$
\mathbb P(X<`r dauer`) = F_{E}(`r dauer`) = 1-\text{e}^{-`r lamm`\cdot `r dauer`} = `r gsub("\\.","\\,",round(pexp(dauer,lam),4))`
$$

```{r, echo=F, fig.cap="Distributional models for continuous random variables",width=640, height=600}
#norm
par(mfrow=c(1,2))

  #exp
  lam<-0.1
  xseq<-seq(0,100,0.01)
  plot(xseq, dexp(xseq,lam),
       type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(0,max(xseq)),
       ylim=c(0,0.5),ylab="",xlab="",main=expression(paste("E(",lambda,"=",0.1,")","  f(x)")))
  #lines(xseq,dexp(xseq,1),col=alpha("gray44",0.5))
  arrows(1/lam,0,1/lam,dexp(1/lam,lam),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  arrows(1/lam,dexp(1/lam,lam),min(xseq),dexp(1/lam,lam),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(floor(min(xseq)),1/lam,0,ceiling(max(xseq))),labels=c("",paste("E(X)=",1/lam),"",""),cex=0.5)
  axis(2,at=c(0,round(dexp(lam,lam),2),0.5),
       labels=c(0,"",0.5),las=2)
  par(xpd=T)
  if(lam!=1){text(-0.01,-0.05,"0",col="gray44")}
  text(22,0.1,paste0("f(x=",lam,")=",round(dexp(lam,lam),2)), col=4)
  #legend("topright",col="gray44",lty=1,legend="E(1)",border="n")
  
  plot(xseq, pexp(xseq,lam),
       type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(0,max(xseq)),
       ylim=c(0,1.1),ylab="",xlab="",main=expression(paste("E(",lambda,"=",0.1,")","  F(x)")))
  arrows(0,1,max(xseq),1,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  #arrows(min(xseq),0.5,mu,0.5,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(0,1/lam, 0,max(xseq)),labels=c(0,"","",""))
  axis(2,at=c(0,0.5,1),labels=c(0,0.5,1),las=2)
  text(1/lam+5,0.4,paste("E(X)=",round(1/lam,2)),col=2)
  text(1/lam+5,0.2,paste("Var(X)=",round(1/lam^2,2)),col=3)
  #lines(xseq,pexp(xseq,1),col=alpha("gray44",0.5))

```

Transactions with long transaction times are suspicious of fraud and may need to be checked manually. You now want to set the threshold for the duration of the transaction: if the threshold is exceeded, the accuracy of the transaction is checked again manually by an employee. However, a maximum of $10% of transactions should be checked.

**How do you choose an appropriate threshold?**

- The approach ($x_0$ is the threshold):
\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb P(X > x_0) = 1-F_E(x_0)= 1-(1-\text e^{-`r lamm` x_0}) = 0.1, 
\end{equation*}

- Solving for $x_0$ gives:

\begin{equation*}
    x_0 =-\ln (0.1)/`r lamm` = `r gsub("\\.","\\,",round(qexp(0.1,lam),4))`\text{ Sekunden}. 
\end{equation*}

- The threshold value should therefore be approximately `r gsub("\\.","\\,",round(qexp(0.1,lam),2))` seconds. 

:::

***

### Uniform distribution `r #emoji::emoji("alien")`

The continuous random variable $X$ is **uniformly distributed** with the parameters $a\in \mathbb R$ and $b\in \mathbb R$, in short $X\sim U(a,b)$, if it has the density
\begin{equation*}
      f(x)=\frac{1}{b-a}, \quad x\in [a,b],
\end{equation*}
(and $f(x)=0$ otherwise).

- The distribution function is $F(x) = \frac{x-a}{b-a} 1_{[a,b]}(x) +1_{(b,\infty)}(x)$.

- Key figures:

\begin{align*}
      \mathbb E(X)&= \int_a^b x\cdot
                       \frac{1}{b-a}\, d x = \frac{a+b}{2},\\
      \text{Var}(X)&=\int_a^b x^2\cdot
                \frac{1}{b-a}\, d x - \frac{(a+b)^2}{4} =
                \frac{(b-a)^2}{12}.
\end{align*}

***

::: {.example #dunif name="Update banner ads"}

In the last example for advertising banners \@ref(exm:dpoisvert) we modeled clicks. Well, advertising banners change and then have to be updated on the SHop website.

You've created a new banner ad template and want to update your ads. A transport script fetches all advertising updates every $20$ minutes. After the transport script picks up your new template, it is immediately online.

- Random variable $Z: \text{'Waiting time in minutes until the web display is updated'}$ follows a continuous uniform distribution on the time interval $[0,20]$: $Z\sim U([0,20])$.

- Expected value and variance:
\begin{align*}
\mathbb E(Z)&=\frac{a+b}2=\frac{20}2=10,\\
\text{Var}(Z)&=\frac{(b-a)^2}{12} = \frac{400}{12}=33\frac13.
\end{align*}

```{r, echo=F, fig.cap="Distributional models for continuous random variables",width=640, height=600}
#norm
par(mfrow=c(1,2))

  #unif
  input$astet<-0
  input$bstet<-20
  plot(c(input$astet,input$bstet), 
       rep(1/(input$bstet -input$astet),2),type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(input$astet-0.1*input$bstet,input$bstet+0.1*input$bstet),
       ylim=c(0,max(0.5,1/(input$bstet - input$astet)+0.1)),ylab="",xlab="",main="U(a,b)  f(x)")
  arrows(input$astet,0,input$astet,1/(input$bstet - input$astet),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  arrows(input$bstet,0,input$bstet,1/(input$bstet - input$astet),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(input$astet,input$bstet),labels=paste(c("a=","b="),c(input$astet,input$bstet)))
  axis(2,at=c(0,1/(input$bstet - input$astet),0.5),labels=c(0,"",0.5),las=2)
  text(10,0.1, paste0("1/(b-a)=",round(1/(input$bstet - input$astet),2)),col=4)
  arrows(5,0.1,-1,0.06,length=0.15,angle=15,col=4)
  
  plot(c(input$astet-0.1*input$bstet,input$astet,input$bstet,input$bstet+0.1*input$bstet), 
       c(0,0,1,1),type="l",col=2,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(input$astet-0.1*input$bstet,input$bstet+0.1*input$bstet),
       ylim=c(0,1.1),ylab="",xlab="",main="U(a,b)   F(x)")
  arrows(input$bstet,0,input$bstet,1,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  arrows(input$astet-0.1*input$bstet,1,input$bstet,1,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(input$astet,round((input$astet+input$bstet)/2,2),input$bstet),labels=paste(c("a=","","b="),c(input$astet,"",input$bstet)))
  axis(2,at=c(0,0.5,1),labels=c(0,0.5,1),las=2)
  par(xpd=T)
  text((input$astet+input$bstet)/2,0.4,paste("E(X)=",round((input$astet+input$bstet)/2,2)),col=2)
  text((input$astet+input$bstet)/2,0.2,paste("Var(X)=",round((input$bstet-input$astet)^2/12,2)),col=3)
  

```
:::

***

::: {#q34 .quiz }

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text318"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer318" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj318)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result318"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj318 = generateQuestion_num(318);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text318').textContent = "You get to the subway station without looking at the clock. The display is broken, but according to a BVG employee, the subways run on time, every 5 minutes. How many minutes will you wait on average?";
    questionObj318.answer = 2.5
```

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text319"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer319" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj319)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result319"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj319 = generateQuestion_num(319);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text319').textContent = "What is the probability that you will catch a subway after just one minute?";
    questionObj319.answer = 0.2
```

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text320"></span>
<input type="number" id="answer320" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj320)">Submit</button></p>
<p id="result320"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj320 = generateQuestion_num(320);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text320').textContent = "Which waiting time is only exceeded in \\(5\\%\\) of cases?";
    questionObj320.answer = 4.75
```

---

<p id="question"> <span id="question-text321"></span></p>
<input type="number" id="answer321" placeholder="Enter your answer">
<button onclick="checkAnswer_num(questionObj321)">Submit</button>
<p id="result321"></p>



```{js,echo=F}

 // Generate a random question
    const questionObj321 = generateQuestion_num(321);
// Display the question
    document.getElementById('question-text321').textContent = "Let the random variable \\(X\\) describe the lifespan of a laptop in hours. The average lifespan of laptops of this type is \\(4000\\) hours. The manufacturer wants to guarantee a minimum number of \\(x_0\\) operating hours, so that only \\(5\\%\\) of the devices need the guarantee service. The manufacturer will in this case guarantee the following amount of operation hours: (Hint: Use exponential distribution. It should hold: \\(\\mathbb P(X\\leq x_0)<0.05.\\) What is \\(x_0\\)?)";
    questionObj321.answer = 205.1732
```

:::



<!--::: {.exercise name="stetige Verteilungen"}

```{r, echo=FALSE,out.width="100%"}
#htmltools::tags$iframe(title = "zusatz_sv", src ="./htmls/zusatz_sv.html",  width=760, height=500)
```

:::-->

***

## Further distributions

### $\chi^2$-distribution

Some distributions are related to each other (such as Bernoulli and binomial distributions). They come about by transforming a random variable with a specific distribution. The $\chi^2$ distribution also represents the distribution of the sum of squared independent standard normally distributed random variables.

This distribution is important for many estimation and test functions such as bpw. $\hat \sigma^2$. Under suitable conditions they have at least an approximate $\chi^2$ distribution.

- Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ be independent and identically $N(0,1)$-distributed random variables. Then the distribution of the random variables is called
\begin{equation*}
      Z=X_1^2 + \cdots + X_n^2
\end{equation*}

- **Chi-square distribution** with $n$ degrees of freedom, short $\chi^2(n)$-distribution, and $Z$ is called $\chi^2$-distributed, short $Z\sim \chi ^2(n)$.

- Parameters:
\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb E(Z)=n\quad\text{ and }\quad \text{Var}(Z)=2n.
\end{equation*}

- Some $\chi^2$ distributions:

```{r,echo=F,fig.cap='Einige Chi-Quadrat-Verteilungen', out.width='80%', fig.asp=.75, fig.align='center'}
  par(mfrow=c(1,2),mar=c(4,6.5,1,1),lwd=2)
  n<-c(10,20,50)
  xseq<-seq(0.1,100,length=1000)
  plot(xseq, dchisq(xseq,n[1]),
       type="l",col=1,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(0,max(xseq)),
       ylim=c(0,max(dchisq(xseq,n[1]))+0.1),ylab="",xlab="",main="f(x)")
  lines(xseq,dchisq(xseq,n[2]),col=2)
  lines(xseq,dchisq(xseq,n[3]),col=4)
  # arrows(1/lam,0,1/lam,dexp(1/lam,lam),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  # arrows(1/lam,dexp(1/lam,lam),min(xseq),dexp(1/lam,lam),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(0,n,100),labels=c(0,paste(n),100))
  axis(2,at=c(0,0.5),las=2)
  # par(xpd=T)
  # if(lam!=1){text(0,-0.2,"0",col="gray44")}
  legend("topright",col=c(1,2,4),lty=1,legend=paste("df=",n),bty="n")

  plot(xseq, pchisq(xseq,n[1]),
       type="l",col=1,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(0,max(xseq)),
       ylim=c(0,1.1),ylab="",xlab="",main="F(x)")
  lines(xseq,pchisq(xseq,n[2]),col=2)
  lines(xseq,pchisq(xseq,n[3]),col=4)
  arrows(0,1,max(xseq),1,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  #arrows(min(xseq),0.5,mu,0.5,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(0,n,100),labels=c(0,paste(n),100))
  axis(2,at=c(0,0.5,1),labels=c(0,0.5,1),las=2)
  # text(1/lam,-0.2,paste("E(X)=",round(1/lam,2)),col=2)
  # text(1/lam+0.2,0.2,paste("Var(X)=",round(1/lam^2,2)),col=3)
  # lines(xseq,pexp(xseq,1),col=alpha("gray44",0.5))

```

- For the estimation we will need quantiles of this distribution. These are calculated using software or taken from the distribution table, where various degrees of freedom can be found in the left column and some probabilities in the top row.


```{r,echo=F,fig.cap='Quantile der Chi-Quadrat-Verteilungen', out.width='80%', fig.asp=.75, fig.align='center'}
#knitr::include_graphics('./images/chi.png')
```

***

::: {.example #chivertmittel name="Stichprobenvarianz von normalverteilten Zufallsvariablen"}

Example \@ref(exm:normvertmittel) cont.

If we also estimate the variance from the sample using the sample variance:

$$
S^2=\frac 1{n-1}\Big((X_1 - \bar X)^2 + \ldots + (X_n - \bar X)^2 \Big)
$$
Then the random variable $\frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma^2}$ is $\chi^2$-distributed with $n-1$ degrees of freedom.

:::

***

### t-distribution

The $t$ distribution is particularly used in parametric tests and confidence intervals. It comes about by dividing a standard normally distributed quantity by the root of a suitably transformed $\chi^2$-distributed random variable.

- Let $X\sim N(0,1)$ and $Z\sim\chi^2(n)$ be independent. Then the distribution of the random variables is called
\begin{equation*}
      T=\frac{X}{\sqrt{Z/n}}
\end{equation*}
**$t$-distributed** with $n$ degrees of freedom, short $T\sim t(n)$.

- Key figures:

\begin{equation*}
      \mathbb E(T)=0 \quad (n>1)\quad\text{ and }\quad
      \text{Var}(T)=\frac{n}{n-2} \quad (n>2).
\end{equation*}

- Some $t$ distributions:

```{r,echo=F,fig.cap='Einige t-Verteilungen', out.width='80%', fig.asp=.75, fig.align='center'}
  par(mfrow=c(1,2),mar=c(4,6.5,1,1),lwd=2)
  n<-c(2,5,20)
  xseq<-seq(-4,4,length=1000)
  plot(xseq, dt(xseq,n[1]),
       type="l",col=1,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(min(xseq),max(xseq)),
       ylim=c(0,0.5),ylab="",xlab="",main="f(x)")
  lines(xseq,dt(xseq,n[2]),col=2)
  lines(xseq,dt(xseq,n[3]),col=4)
  lines(xseq,dnorm(xseq,0,1),col=scales::alpha("gray44",0.5))
  # arrows(1/lam,0,1/lam,dexp(1/lam,lam),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  # arrows(1/lam,dexp(1/lam,lam),min(xseq),dexp(1/lam,lam),col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(-4,0,4),labels=c("",0,""))
  axis(2,at=c(0,0.5),las=2)
  # par(xpd=T)
  # if(lam!=1){text(0,-0.2,"0",col="gray44")}
  legend("topright",col=c(1,2,4,"gray44"),lty=1,legend=c(paste("df=",n),"N(0,1)"),bty="n")

  plot(xseq, pt(xseq,n[1]),
       type="l",col=1,axes=F,lwd=2,
       xlim=c(min(xseq),max(xseq)),
       ylim=c(0,1.1),ylab="",xlab="",main="F(x)")
  lines(xseq,pt(xseq,n[2]),col=2)
  lines(xseq,pt(xseq,n[3]),col=4)
  arrows(min(xseq),1,max(xseq),1,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  lines(xseq,pnorm(xseq,0,1),col=scales::alpha("gray44",0.5))
  #arrows(min(xseq),0.5,mu,0.5,col=4,lty=2,code=0)
  axis(1,at=c(-4,0,4),labels=c("",0,""))
  axis(2,at=c(0,0.5,1),labels=c(0,0.5,1),las=2)
  # text(1/lam,-0.2,paste("E(X)=",round(1/lam,2)),col=2)
  # text(1/lam+0.2,0.2,paste("Var(X)=",round(1/lam^2,2)),col=3)
  # lines(xseq,pexp(xseq,1),col=alpha("gray44",0.5))

```


- The $t$ distribution has more distribution mass in the tails (“heavy tails”) compared to the normal distribution.


- For $n\rightarrow\infty$, the density function of the $t$ distribution converges to the density of the standard normal distribution.

- For the estimation we will need quantiles of this distribution. These are calculated using software or taken from the distribution table, where various degrees of freedom can be found in the left column and some probabilities in the top row.


```{r,echo=F,fig.cap='Quantile der t-Verteilung', out.width='80%', fig.asp=.75, fig.align='center'}
#knitr::include_graphics('./images/t.png')
```

***

::: {.example #tvertmittel name="Sample mean of normally distributed random variables"}

Continuation of examples \@ref(exm:normvertmittel) and \@ref(exm:chivertmittel).

In the example above we saw that under the normal distribution assumption:

- $\frac{\bar X-\mu_X}{\frac{\sigma_X}{\sqrt n}}\sim N(0;1)$.

But if $\sigma_X^2$ is unknown and is estimated, one replaces the true standard deviation in standardization with the estimate $S$ ($S_X=\sqrt{S_X^2}$, $S_X^2$ is the sample variance) , then:

- $\frac{\bar X-\mu_X}{\frac{S_X}{\sqrt n}}\sim t(n-1)$.

- Calculations$^\ast$:

\[\begin{align}
\frac{\bar X-\mu_X}{\frac{S_X}{\sqrt n}} &= \frac{\overbrace{\frac{\bar X-\mu_X}{\sigma_X/\sqrt n}}^{N(0,1)}}{\underbrace{\sqrt\frac{(n-1)S^2_X}{\sigma^2_X}}_{()^2\sim \chi^2(n-1)}/\sqrt{n-1}}\sim t(n-1).
\end{align}\]


So a standard normally distributed ZV $\frac{\bar /\sigma_X/(n-1)$ (divided by the degrees of freedom of the latter) and by definition of the $t$ distribution $\frac{\bar X-\mu_X}{\frac{S_X}{\sqrt n} So }$ is $t$-distributed with $n-1$ degrees of freedom.

:::

***

::: {.example #tlinreg name="Linear regression under normal distribution assumption`r #emoji::emoji("alien")`"}

Example \@ref(exm:normvertlinreg) cont.

- Since $\sigma^2$ - the variance of the unobservable error terms $\varepsilon_i$ is always estimated by $\hat\sigma^2 = \frac 1{n-2}\sum_{i=1}^n\big (Y_i - (\hat \alpha + \hat \beta x_i)\big)^2$ ($n-2$ comes from the fact that we now estimate two parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ and $2$ degrees of freedom as a result lose) and

- The variances of the estimators $\sigma_{\hat\alpha}^2$ and $\sigma_{\hat\beta}^2$ are based on this and must therefore also be determined by the estimates $\hat\sigma_{\hat\alpha }^2$ and $\hat\sigma_{\hat\beta}^2$ are replaced, the following applies:

$$
\frac{\hat\beta - \beta}{\hat\sigma_{\hat\beta}}\sim t(n-2)\,\
\frac{\hat\alpha - \alpha}{\hat\sigma_{\hat\alpha}}\sim t(n-2).
$$

These derived distributions will be useful to us later when we quantify the uncertainty associated with an estimate.

:::

***


<!--::: {.exercise name="Continuous distributions"}

```{r, echo=FALSE,out.width="100%"}
#htmltools::tags$iframe(title = "quiz_sv", src ="./htmls/quiz_sv.html",  width=760, height=500)
```

:::-->

```{r, echo=FALSE,out.width="100%"}
htmltools::tags$iframe(title = "Wortwolke", src =paste0(link,"Apps_RBook/#section-wortwolke"),  width=760, height=600)
```
-->
